text:
Every February 30th — a date that exists nowhere on the calendar and therefore everywhere in the heart — we observe the Festival of Tomorrow's Tomorrow, a holiday devoted entirely to things we have meant to do but have not yet done. It is the world's only celebration that requires no celebration, since the proper way to honor it is to postpone it.

The morning begins with a hearty breakfast of intentions, served lukewarm, because nobody got around to reheating them. Families gather to read aloud from their to-do lists, weeping gently at the ambitious person they once planned to become. Children write letters to their future selves and then forget to mail them, which is considered the highest form of piety.

The traditional gift is an unassembled object — a bookshelf, a model ship, a relationship — exchanged with the solemn promise that it will be completed "soon." Guests admire one another's procrastination with genuine reverence. "You've been meaning to learn the cello for eleven years?" one might murmur. "How devout."

At dusk, everyone gathers to not finish the things they started, and a single candle is lit, though the lighting is usually delegated to someone else who eventually does it next week. The festival closes with the singing of the anthem, whose lyrics were never written, so the crowd simply hums the melody it always intended to compose.

By midnight the holiday is over, having technically never occurred. We return to our ordinary days quietly changed, comforted by the deep and human truth that there is always, mercifully, tomorrow.
